title: Woven wall hanging with gradient weft
subtitle: A frame loom wall hanging using a stepped colour gradient and rya knot fringe, approximately 30 x 40 cm
excerpt: This frame loom wall hanging uses a stepped two-colour gradient as the main body, with rya knot fringe at the lower edge for texture. The gradient is built by alternating rows of the two weft colours in a decreasing ratio, which produces a smooth ombré effect visible across the hanging.
sourceNotes: Frame loom wall hanging technique is documented consistently across beginner weaving instruction. Rya knotting on frame looms is taught in UK guild courses and in published craft instruction. The gradient ratio sequence is synthesised from standard colour-blending methods.

glossaryTerms.definition: A colour technique in which the weft gradually shifts from one colour to another across a woven piece by alternating rows of two colours in a changing ratio. Starting with all colour A and gradually increasing colour B rows produces a smooth ombré or gradient effect.

A Scandinavian pile knot tied around pairs of warp threads to create a loop or cut fringe that stands proud of the woven surface. In wall hangings, rya knots are woven in rows and the loops cut to produce a shaggy, textured fringe or decorative pile.

The side edges of a woven cloth where the weft turns back at the end of each row. In a wall hanging, clean selvedges are formed by leaving a slight arc of weft in each row before beating down, preventing the edge warp threads from being pulled inward.

content.text: technique requires no additional tools or complex shedding: the gradient is built simply by changing the proportion of two weft colours across successive rows. A row of
woven into the lower edge adds a fringe that frames the hanging. Clean
are the main technical skill to develop; the rest of the work is straightforward plain weave.

items.name: Cotton warp thread, natural or white, approximately 30 m
Weft yarn colour A (e.g. deep terracotta), DK weight, 40 m
Weft yarn colour B (e.g. pale blush), DK weight, 40 m
Additional yarn for rya knots: DK or aran weight, any colour, 20 m
Frame loom, minimum 30 cm wide and 45 cm tall
Wooden dowel or branch, at least 35 cm wide, for mounting
Fork or comb for beating down weft rows

content.text: Warp the frame loom with cotton thread at 4 to 5 ends per cm across a 30 cm width. Wind the warp continuously around the top and bottom bars of the frame, keeping the tension even on every pass. Weave 4 rows of scrap yarn at the bottom to space the warp evenly and create a base before starting the main hanging.
Step 2: Weave the rya knot row

Cut the rya knot yarn into lengths of approximately 20 cm. To tie each rya knot: fold a length in half, place the fold behind two adjacent warp threads, then bring both tails forward over the outside of those same warp threads and between them. Pull firmly. Work rya knots across the full width in pairs of warp threads. Once all knots are tied, weave two rows of plain weave in colour A to lock the knots in place before continuing with the gradient body. The knot tails hang free below the warp; these will become the fringe.

Build the gradient using the following row ratio sequence, working upward from the rya knot row: 8 rows all colour A; 6 rows A, 2 rows B; 4 rows A, 4 rows B; 2 rows A, 6 rows B; 8 rows all colour B. Weave each colour in its own rows rather than blending within a row. Leave a slight arc of weft in every row before beating with the fork to maintain even selvedges. The two-needle approach (one needle per colour) avoids cutting and re-joining yarn between colour changes.

When the weaving reaches the desired height, weave 4 rows of scrap yarn to hold the structure, then cut the warp threads off the frame, leaving at least 15 cm of warp at the top. Thread each top warp end onto a blunt darning needle and weave it back into the cloth for 3 to 4 cm to hide the end; repeat for all top ends. Cut the rya knot fringe to an even length at the bottom. Thread the top of the hanging onto a wooden dowel by looping each warp end over the dowel, or attach the dowel with a row of half-hitches. Hang from a cord tied at both ends of the dowel.

items.problem: The sides of the hanging curve inward and the width is narrowing as weaving progresses
items.cause: The weft is being pulled tight against the edge warp threads at the selvedge
items.fix: After each pass, use a finger to push a small arc of weft at both edges before pressing the row down with the fork. The arc allows the weft to wrap around the outer warp threads without drawing them inward.

items.problem: The gradient transition looks abrupt rather than smooth
items.cause: The ratio change between colour A and colour B rows was too large between sections
items.fix: Add intermediate sections between each ratio jump: for example, add a section of 5 rows A and 3 rows B between the 6/2 and 4/4 sections. The more intermediate steps, the smoother the gradient.
